subject: Physics
q: A sand dropper drops sand of mass $m(t)$ on a conveyer belt at a rate proportional to the square root of speed $(v)$ of the belt, i.e. $\frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \sqrt{v}$. If P is the power delivered to run the belt at constant speed then which of the following relationship is true?
question: A sand dropper drops sand of mass $m(t)$ on a conveyer belt at a rate proportional to the square root of speed $(v)$ of the belt, i.e. $\frac{\mathrm{dm}}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto \sqrt{v}$. If P is the power delivered to run the belt at constant speed then which of the following relationship is true?
solution:
Power $=\vec{F} \cdot \vec{V}$
and $F=\frac{d p}{d t}=$ Rate of change of linear momentum
$F=V \cdot \frac{d m}{d t}=K_1 V^{\frac{3}{2}}, K$ is constant
Power $(P)=\left(K V^{\frac{3}{2}}\right) \cdot(V)$
$=K V^{\frac{5}{2}}$

So, $P^2 \propto V^5$

subject: Physics
q: If momentum of a body is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, then its kinetic energy increases by :
question: If momentum of a body is increased by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo>%</mo></math>, then its kinetic energy increases by :
solution:
A body of mass m, moving with momentum p and kinetic energy K are related as follows,
K=p22m.
As the momentum of the body is increased by 20%, then its momentum becomes,
p+20100p=1.2 p
and the mass of the body remains unchanged. So, kinetic energy is directly related to the square of momentum. 

Hence, new kinetic energy K' will be,
K'=1.2 p22m

K'=1.44 K.

So, the percentage change in kinetic energy
=K'-KK×100 
=1.44K-KK×100
=1.44-1×100
=44%.

subject: Physics
q: If the kinetic energy of a moving body becomes four times its initial kinetic energy, then the percentage change in its momentum will be:
question: If the kinetic energy of a moving body becomes four times its initial kinetic energy, then the percentage change in its momentum will be:
solution:
K2=4 K1

12mv22=4×12mv12

v2=2v1

P=mv

P2=mv2=2mv1

P1=mv1

% change =ΔPP1×100=2mv1-mv1mv1×100=100%

subject: Physics
q: A small bob $A$ of mass $m$ is attached to a massless rigid rod of length 1 m pivoted at point $P$ and kept at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with vertical as shown in figure. At distance of 1 m below point $P$, an identical bob $B$ is kept at rest on a smooth horizontal surface that extends to a circular track of radius $R$ as shown in figure. If bob $B$ just manages to complete the circular path of radius $R$ upto a point $Q$ after being hit elastically by $\operatorname{bob} A$, then radius $R$ is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
question: A small bob $A$ of mass $m$ is attached to a massless rigid rod of length 1 m pivoted at point $P$ and kept at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with vertical as shown in figure. At distance of 1 m below point $P$, an identical bob $B$ is kept at rest on a smooth horizontal surface that extends to a circular track of radius $R$ as shown in figure. If bob $B$ just manages to complete the circular path of radius $R$ upto a point $Q$ after being hit elastically by $\operatorname{bob} A$, then radius $R$ is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
solution:
Bob A swings from $60°$ to vertical. Height drop $h = l(1-\cos 60°) = 1 \times 0.5 = 0.5$ m.
Velocity of A at bottom: $v_A = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 0.5} = \sqrt{10}$ m/s.
Elastic collision between equal masses: B gets $v_B = \sqrt{10}$ m/s, A stops.
For B to just complete circular loop: $v_B^2 = 5gR$.
$10 = 5 \times 10 \times R \Rightarrow R = \frac{1}{5}$ m.

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass (5±0.5) kg is moving with a velocity of (20±0.4) m s-1. Its kinetic energy will be
question: A body of mass (5±0.5) kg is moving with a velocity of (20±0.4) m s-1. Its kinetic energy will be
solution:
The kinetic energy is given by,

K=12mv2

Differentiating the above equation,

dK=12(v2dm+2mvdv)

Dividing by K,

⇒dKK=dmm+2dvv

⇒dK1000=0.55+2×0.420

⇒dK=100+40=140

⇒KE=(1000±140)J

subject: Physics
q: The rain drop of mass $1$ g, starts with zero velocity from a height of $1$ km. It hits the ground with a speed of $5$ m/s. The work done by the unknown resistive force is _______ J. (take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: The rain drop of mass $1$ g, starts with zero velocity from a height of $1$ km. It hits the ground with a speed of $5$ m/s. The work done by the unknown resistive force is _______ J. (take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Using the work-energy theorem, the total work done by all forces equals the change in kinetic energy:

$W_{g} + W_{r} = \Delta K$

$mgh + W_{r} = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2 - 0$

Substituting the given values ($m = 10^{-3}$ kg, $g = 10$ m/s$^2$, $h = 1000$ m, $v = 5$ m/s):

$(10^{-3})(10)(1000) + W_{r} = \dfrac{1}{2}(10^{-3})(5)^2$

$10 + W_{r} = 0.0125$

$W_{r} = 0.0125 - 10 = -9.9875$ J

$W_{r} \approx -9.98$ J

Answer: $-9.98$

subject: Physics
q:
A block of mass 5 kg starting from rest pulled up on a smooth incline plane making an angle of 30° with horizontal with an effective acceleration of 1 m s-2. The power delivered by the puling force at t=10 s from the start is _____ W.
[Use g=10 m s-2]
(Calculate the nearest integer value)
question:
A block of mass 5 kg starting from rest pulled up on a smooth incline plane making an angle of 30° with horizontal with an effective acceleration of 1 m s-2. The power delivered by the puling force at t=10 s from the start is _____ W.
[Use g=10 m s-2]
(Calculate the nearest integer value)
solution:
Let's consider the following free-body diagram:

From the above diagram, it can be written that

F-mgsin30°=ma   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the force applied on the block.

F-50 sin30°=5×1⇒F=5+25 N=30 N

The velocity of the block v at t=10 s can be written as

v=0+1 m s-2×10 s=10 m s-1

Hence, the required power is given by

P=Fv= 30 N×10 m s-1= 300 W

subject: Physics
q: A body is dropped on ground from a height h1 and after hitting the ground, it rebounds to a height h2. If the ratio of velocities of the body just before and after hitting ground is 4, then percentage loss in kinetic energy of the body is x4. The value of x is _____.
question: A body is dropped on ground from a height h1 and after hitting the ground, it rebounds to a height h2. If the ratio of velocities of the body just before and after hitting ground is 4, then percentage loss in kinetic energy of the body is x4. The value of x is _____.
solution:
The given ratio of the velocities is 

v1v2=4

The percentage loss in kinetic energy is 

mv212-mv222mv212×100=x4⇒v21-v22v21=x400⇒1-v22v21=x400⇒1-116=x400⇒x=375

subject: Physics
q: For a particle in uniform circular motion, the acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at any point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> on the circular path of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is (when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is measured from the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> is uniform speed):
question: For a particle in uniform circular motion, the acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi>a</mi><mo>→</mo></mover></math> at any point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> on the circular path of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is (when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is measured from the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> is uniform speed):
solution:
Acceleration of a particle in uniform circular motion is directed towards the centre of the circle and its magnitude is v2R. The radius vector isr→=Rcosθi^+Rsinθj^

Therefore, a→=-v2Rr^=v2R−cosθi^−sinθj^

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> mass takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> to reach ground when dropped from a certain height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> above surface of earth. The loss of potential energy in the last second of fall is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> mass takes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> to reach ground when dropped from a certain height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> above surface of earth. The loss of potential energy in the last second of fall is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. [Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>]
solution:
Displacement in nth second is given by Sn=u+a22n-1

Here, displacement is 8th second is

S8=0+12×10×(2×8-1)

⇒S8=75 m

Loss of potential energy is 

ΔU=mgS8=0.4×10×75

⇒ΔU=300 J.

subject: Physics
q: A spherical ball of mass $2$ kg falls from a height of $10$ m and is brought to rest after penetrating $10$ cm into sand. The average force exerted by sand on the ball is _______ N. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: A spherical ball of mass $2$ kg falls from a height of $10$ m and is brought to rest after penetrating $10$ cm into sand. The average force exerted by sand on the ball is _______ N. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
Using the work-energy theorem, the total work done on the ball is equal to its change in kinetic energy.

Since the ball starts from rest and finally comes to rest, the change in kinetic energy is zero.

$W_{\text{gravity}} + W_{\text{sand}} = \Delta K = 0$

The work done by gravity is over the total distance $h + d$, and the work done by the resistive force of the sand $F$ is over the distance $d$.

$mg(h + d) - Fd = 0$

$F = \dfrac{mg(h + d)}{d}$

Given $m = 2$ kg, $g = 10$ m/s$^2$, $h = 10$ m, and $d = 10$ cm $= 0.1$ m:

$F = \dfrac{2 \times 10 \times (10 + 0.1)}{0.1}$

$F = \dfrac{20 \times 10.1}{0.1}$

$F = 20 \times 101 = 2020$ N

Answer: $2020$

subject: Physics
q: The bob of a pendulum was released from a horizontal position. The length of the pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If it dissipates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of its initial energy against air resistance, the speed with which the bob arrives at the lowest point is: [Use, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ]
question: The bob of a pendulum was released from a horizontal position. The length of the pendulum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. If it dissipates <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>%</mo></math> of its initial energy against air resistance, the speed with which the bob arrives at the lowest point is: [Use, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> ]
solution:
Given the length of the pendulum is ℓ=10 m.

The initial potential energy of the pendulum at the horizontal position is given by

U=mgℓ   ...1

The final kinetic energy of the pendulum at the lowermost position is given by

K=12mv2   ...2

So, from equations (1) and (2), it follows that

910mgℓ=12mv2⇒910×10×10=12v2⇒v2=180⇒v=180=65  m s-1

q:
A uniform chain of length 3 m and mass 3 kg overhangs a smooth table with 2 m laying on the table. If K is the kinetic energy of the chain in J as it completely slips off the table, then the value of K is

question:

Take g=10 m s-2
solution:
Apply conservation of energy Ui+Ki=Uf+Kf

−1×10×12+0=−3×10×1.5+KE

K.E.=45−5

K.E.=40 J

question: Hydrogen ion and singly ionized helium atom are accelerated, from rest, through the same potential difference. The ratio of final speeds of hydrogen and helium ions is close to:

subject: Physics
q: A wedge of mass <math><mi>M</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>m</mi></math> lies on a frictionless plane. A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> approaches the wedge with speed <math><mi>v</mi><mo>.</mo></math> There is no friction between the particle and the plane or between the particle and the wedge. The maximum height climbed by the particle on the wedge is given by:
question: A wedge of mass <math><mi>M</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>m</mi></math> lies on a frictionless plane. A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> approaches the wedge with speed <math><mi>v</mi><mo>.</mo></math> There is no friction between the particle and the plane or between the particle and the wedge. The maximum height climbed by the particle on the wedge is given by:
solution:
From conservation of linear momentum,

mv=4m+mvf
vf=v5
From conservation of mechanical energy,
12mv2=124m+mvf2+mgh
v2=5vf2+2gh
v2=5×v52+2gh
h=2v25g

subject: Physics
q:
A ball of mass 100 g is dropped from a height h=10 cm on a platform fixed at the top of a vertical spring (as shown in figure). The ball stays on the platform and the platform is depressed by a distance h2. The spring constant is _____ N m-1
(Use g=10 m s-2)
question:
A ball of mass 100 g is dropped from a height h=10 cm on a platform fixed at the top of a vertical spring (as shown in figure). The ball stays on the platform and the platform is depressed by a distance h2. The spring constant is _____ N m-1
(Use g=10 m s-2)
solution:
Loss in the gravitational potential energy of the ball= gain in the potential energy by the spring

-∆PEgravitational=∆PEspring

mgh+h2=12kh22⇒mg3h2=kh28

⇒k=12mgh=12×0.1×100.1=120 N m-1

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> block moving at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> compresses a spring through a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> when its speed is halved. The spring constant of the spring will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> block moving at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>ms</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> compresses a spring through a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> when its speed is halved. The spring constant of the spring will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Nm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>
solution:
Given: Mass of the block m=500 g, initial velocity of the block u=12 m s-1, and final velocity of the block v=u2=6 m s-1.

As non-conservative forces are not acting on the system we can conserve energy.

Thus, loss in kinetic energy of block = Gain in potential energy of spring

12mu2-12mv2=12kx2

12×0.5×122-12×0.5×62=12k×0.32

k=600 N m-1

subject: Physics
q: A small particle moves to position <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> from its initial position <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> under the action of force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of work done will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
question: A small particle moves to position <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> from its initial position <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> under the action of force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>7</mn><mover><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. The value of work done will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
Work done by a constant force is given by W=ForceF→·Displacementr→, where, r→=r→2-r→1

Given here, r1→=5i^-2j^+k^ and r→2=2i^+3j^-4k^

So, W=F→·r→2-r→1

=(5i^+2j^+7k^)·(5i^-2j^+k^)-(2i^+3j^-4k^)

⇒W=40 J

subject: Physics
q: Potential energy ($V$) versus distance ($x$) is given by the graph. Rank various regions as per the magnitudes of the force (F) acting on a particle from high to low.
question: Potential energy ($V$) versus distance ($x$) is given by the graph. Rank various regions as per the magnitudes of the force (F) acting on a particle from high to low.
solution:
Force is related to potential energy by $F = -\frac{dV}{dx}$.
Magnitude of force $|F| = \left|\frac{dV}{dx}\right|$ = |slope of V-x graph|
From graph coordinates: A(1, 0), B(1.5, 1), C(2, 4), D(4, 4), E(5, 3)
$|F_{AB}| = \frac{1-0}{1.5-1} = 2$
$|F_{BC}| = \frac{4-1}{2-1.5} = 6$
$|F_{CD}| = \frac{4-4}{4-2} = 0$
$|F_{DE}| = \frac{|3-4|}{5-4} = 1$
Ranking: $F_{BC} > F_{AB} > F_{DE} > F_{CD}$

subject: Physics
q: Two particles having masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> respectively are moving with equal kinetic energies. The ratio of the magnitudes of their linear momentum is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> will be ___.

The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> will be ___.
solution:
p122×4=p222×16

p1p2=12

subject: Physics
q: A force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> acts on a particle in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> direction where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is in Newton and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is in meter. The work done by this force during a displacement from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
question: A force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> acts on a particle in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> direction where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math> is in Newton and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is in meter. The work done by this force during a displacement from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mo>=</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>.
solution:
The formula to calculate the work done by a variable force is given by

W=∫x1x2Fdx   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required work done.

W=∫04 (2+3x)dx=2x+32x204=[8+24] J=32 J

subject: Physics
q: A bob of mass $m$ is suspended at a point $O$ by a light string of length $l$ and left to perform vertical motion (circular) as shown in figure. Initially, by applying horizontal velocity $v_0$ at the point ' A ', the string becomes slack when, the bob reaches at the point ' D '. The ratio of the kinetic energy of the bob at the points $B$ and $C$ is ______ -.
question: A bob of mass $m$ is suspended at a point $O$ by a light string of length $l$ and left to perform vertical motion (circular) as shown in figure. Initially, by applying horizontal velocity $v_0$ at the point ' A ', the string becomes slack when, the bob reaches at the point ' D '. The ratio of the kinetic energy of the bob at the points $B$ and $C$ is ______ -.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{A}}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{B}}^2+\mathrm{mgh} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}(5 \mathrm{~g} \ell)=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{B}}^2+\mathrm{mg} \frac{\ell}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{5 \mathrm{mg} \ell}{2}-\frac{\mathrm{mg} \ell}{2}=\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{B}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{mg} \ell \\ & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{C}}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\mathrm{D}}^2+\mathrm{mg} \frac{\ell}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mg} \ell+\mathrm{mg} \frac{\ell}{2}=\mathrm{mg} \ell \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{C}}}=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: If the maximum load carried by an elevator is 1400 kg (600 kg-Passengers +800 kg-elevator) , which is moving up with a uniform speed of 3 m s-1 and the frictional force acting on it is 2000 N, then the maximum power used by the motor is _____________kW. g=10 m s-2
question: If the maximum load carried by an elevator is 1400 kg (600 kg-Passengers +800 kg-elevator) , which is moving up with a uniform speed of 3 m s-1 and the frictional force acting on it is 2000 N, then the maximum power used by the motor is _____________kW. g=10 m s-2
solution:
The maximum force acting is, Fmax=14000+2000 N

= 16000 N

The formula for power is given by, P=Fmaxv.

It is given, v=3 m s-1.

⇒P = 16000 N × 3 m s-1 = 48000 W=48 kW

⇒W = 48 kW

subject: Physics
q: The potential energy function for the force between two atoms in a diatomic molecule is approximately given by $U(x)=\frac{a}{x^{12}}-\frac{b}{x^6}$, where a and $b$ are constants and $x$ is the distance between the atoms. If the dissociation energy of the molecule is $D=\left[U(x=\infty)-U_{\text {at equilbrium }}\right], D$ is
question: The potential energy function for the force between two atoms in a diatomic molecule is approximately given by $U(x)=\frac{a}{x^{12}}-\frac{b}{x^6}$, where a and $b$ are constants and $x$ is the distance between the atoms. If the dissociation energy of the molecule is $D=\left[U(x=\infty)-U_{\text {at equilbrium }}\right], D$ is
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& U(x)=\frac{a}{x^{12}}-\frac{b}{x^6} \\
& U(x=\infty)=0
\end{aligned}
$$
as, $\quad \mathrm{F}=-\frac{\mathrm{dU}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\left[\frac{12 \mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{x}^{13}}+\frac{6 \mathrm{~b}}{\mathrm{x}^7}\right]$ at equilibrium, $\quad F=0$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & x^6=\frac{2 a}{b} \\ \therefore & U_{\text {at equiltrium }}=\frac{a}{\left(\frac{2 a}{b}\right)^2}-\frac{b}{\left(\frac{2 a}{b}\right)}=\frac{-b^2}{4 a} \\ \therefore & D=\left[U(x=\infty)-U_{\text {at equilibium }}\right]=\frac{b^2}{4 a}\end{array}$

subject: Physics
q: A wire fixed at the upper end stretches by length by applying a force F. The work done in stretching is
question: A wire fixed at the upper end stretches by length by applying a force F. The work done in stretching is
solution:
Work done $=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kx}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{k} \ell^2$ where $\ell$ is the total extensions.
$$
=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{k} \ell) \ell=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~F} \ell
$$

subject: Physics
q: A force $\mathrm{f}=\mathrm{x}^2 \mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{y}^2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$ acts on a particle in a plane $\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}=10$. The work done by this force during a displacement from $(0,0)$ to $(4 \mathrm{~m}, 2 \mathrm{~m})$ is $\qquad$ Joule (round off to the nearest integer)
question: A force $\mathrm{f}=\mathrm{x}^2 \mathrm{y} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{y}^2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$ acts on a particle in a plane $\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}=10$. The work done by this force during a displacement from $(0,0)$ to $(4 \mathrm{~m}, 2 \mathrm{~m})$ is $\qquad$ Joule (round off to the nearest integer)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & y=10-x \\ & w=\int_0^4 x^2(10-x) d x+\int_0^2 y^2 d y \\ & =\frac{10 x^3}{3}-\left.\frac{x^4}{4}\right|_0 ^4+\left.\frac{y^3}{3}\right|_0 ^2 \\ & =\frac{640}{3}-\frac{256}{4}+\frac{8}{3} \\ & =216 \times 64 \\ & =152 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is driven by an engine delivering a constant power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. the body starts from rest and moves in a straight line. After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the body has moved a distance (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>)….
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is driven by an engine delivering a constant power of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. the body starts from rest and moves in a straight line. After <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, the body has moved a distance (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>)….
solution:
Given,

Power delivered by the engine P=1 J s-1

Mass of the body is m= 2 kg 

Body starts from the rest, u=0

Using work energy Theorem, 

W=∆K=Pt

Pt=12mv2-0

Substitute the values in the above equation, we get
(1)t=12×2×v2
⇒v=t=dsdt
⇒∫0tds=∫09tdt

⇒s=93232=23×27

⇒s=18 m

subject: Physics
q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is moving in a circular path of constant radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> such that its centripetal acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mi>c</mi></msub></math> is varying with time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mi>c</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>r</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> , where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is a constant. The power delivered to the particle by the force acting on it is -
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> is moving in a circular path of constant radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> such that its centripetal acceleration <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mi>c</mi></msub></math> is varying with time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mi>c</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>r</mi><msup><mi>t</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> , where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is a constant. The power delivered to the particle by the force acting on it is -
solution:
ac=k2rt2

Or v2r=k2rt2  Or    v=krt
Therefore, tangential acceleration, at=dvdt=kr

Therefore, tangential force, Ft=mat=mkr

Only tangential force does work.

Power=Ftv=mkrkrt ⇒Power=mk2r2t

subject: Physics
q: A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving horizontally with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> hits a wooden block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> kept on a horizontal rough surface. The bullet gets embedded into the block and moves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before coming to rest. The coefficient of friction between the block and the surface is _______.
question: A bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving horizontally with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> hits a wooden block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> kept on a horizontal rough surface. The bullet gets embedded into the block and moves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> before coming to rest. The coefficient of friction between the block and the surface is _______.
solution:
Let the mass of the bullet be m and mass of the block be M. It is given that V=400 m s-1. By conservation of momentum,

mV=(m+M)v

 ⇒0.1×400=(0.1+3.9)v
⇒v=10 m s-1

The force acting on the block 

f=μm'g⇒a=μg

Using  v'2-v2=-2aS

⇒ 02-102=2(-μg)(S)
⇒μ=1002×10×20=0.25

The door is $1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ wide and weighs $12 \mathrm{~kg}$. It is hinged at one end and rotates about a vertical axis practically without friction. solution: No solution is given.
